skill.name: image-edit
skill.description: Route image edit requests to the right RunComfy model for batch edits, text replacement, multi-reference composition, local edits, and mask-based inpainting.
skill.summary: Edit images through RunComfy with intent-based model routing.
skill.author: agentspace-so

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, and weak-crypto patterns, but review shows most high alerts are Markdown examples or placeholder URLs. The real risks are legitimate for this skill: it instructs agents to run the RunComfy CLI, transmit prompts and image URLs to RunComfy services, store or read a RunComfy token, and write generated files locally.

medium_findings.title: Intentional RunComfy CLI Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to install and run the RunComfy CLI and provides multiple runcomfy run examples. This is expected behavior, but it means users should review generated command arguments and output paths before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly documents local CLI execution as its primary operation. The commands are product-specific and not malicious, but external command execution is a real operational risk.

medium_findings.title: Outbound Model API and Remote Asset Transfer
medium_findings.description: The skill sends prompts and image or mask URLs to RunComfy model services. This is required for the service, but users may expose private images, text, masks, or external asset URLs to a third-party processor.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation states that the CLI posts to the Model API and downloads generated outputs. This confirms network transfer, but not malicious exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Token Storage and Local Output Writes
medium_findings.description: The skill documents token storage under the user configuration directory and writes generated outputs to a selected output directory. This requires normal filesystem access and should be disclosed to users.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file names the token path and output directory behavior directly. The documented 0600 mode and size cap reduce risk, but filesystem access remains material.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Ruby Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The repeated Ruby or shell backtick findings occur in Markdown prose, inline code, tables, and fenced bash examples. No Ruby source file or executable backtick operator was found in the reviewed skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only reviewed file is Markdown, and the flagged patterns are formatting delimiters or command examples. They do not execute by themselves.

low_findings.title: Placeholder URLs and Paths Misclassified as Traversal
low_findings.description: The path traversal alerts are caused by placeholder HTTPS URLs and placeholder output directory text in documentation. No evidence found of code that resolves user paths or traverses directories.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious sequences are inside example URLs such as https://... and placeholders such as <absolute/path>. No executable path handling code is present.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Lack Supporting Evidence
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts point to metadata, prose, or section headings. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, or other weak cryptographic code in the reviewed skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are description text, a related skill name, and an Exit codes heading. They do not implement cryptography.

dangerous_patterns.title: External CLI Invocation Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill routes tasks to runcomfy run commands. This is legitimate for RunComfy automation, but agents should avoid adding shell metacharacters or unreviewed local paths when constructing commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is repeated across documented invocations and is central to the skill. The risk is controlled by careful JSON argument construction.

dangerous_patterns.title: Third-Party Image Processing Boundary
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow sends image URLs, mask URLs, prompts, and generated output retrieval requests through RunComfy endpoints. Users should not submit confidential assets unless this processing is acceptable.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The security section explicitly describes API submission, output downloads, and third-party URL fetching. This is a confirmed data boundary issue rather than hidden behavior.

content.user_title: Edit Images with RunComfy Model Routing
content.value_statement: Choosing the right image edit model can waste time and credits. This skill routes each edit request to a suitable RunComfy model and gives model-specific prompt patterns.
actual_capabilities: Routes general and batch edits to Nano Banana Edit by default.
Routes multilingual text replacement and multi-reference composition to GPT Image 2 Edit.
Routes single-image local edits to Flux Kontext Pro.
Routes mask-based object removal and region replacement to Z-Image Turbo Inpaint.
Provides RunComfy CLI command patterns with model-specific input fields.
Documents prompt guidance for preservation, spatial scope, masks, and batch consistency.
limitations: Requires a RunComfy account and local RunComfy CLI access.
Uses publicly fetchable HTTPS image and mask URLs for model inputs.
Runs one selected model route per call, not blended multi-model pipelines.
Quality and supported options depend on the selected RunComfy model.

use_cases.title: Batch Product Image Cleanup
use_cases.description: Create consistent SKU edits by locking aspect ratio and resolution across multiple product images.

use_cases.title: Localized Campaign Adaptation
use_cases.description: Replace in-image headlines with multilingual text while preserving the original layout and brand marks.

use_cases.title: Precise Object Removal
use_cases.description: Use mask-based inpainting to remove cables, watermarks, or background distractions from selected regions.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Background Swap
prompt_templates.prompt: Edit this image with RunComfy. Keep the subject identity, pose, and clothing unchanged. Replace only the background with [new background]. Use the best route.

prompt_templates.title: Batch Consistent Product Edit
prompt_templates.prompt: Edit these product images as a consistent batch. Keep the product unchanged. Change [specific element], lock aspect ratio to [ratio], and use [resolution] output.

prompt_templates.title: Multilingual Text Replacement
prompt_templates.prompt: Replace only the in-image text with "[exact translated text]" in [script or language]. Keep the photograph, layout, logo, and font weight unchanged.

prompt_templates.title: Masked Region Inpainting
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this image and mask to replace the masked region with [replacement]. Preserve all unmasked areas, match lighting, and use strength [value].

output_examples.input: User asks to remove overhead cables from a street photo and provides a mask URL.
output_examples.output: The skill selects Z-Image Turbo Inpaint, prepares the image and mask inputs, and recommends a lower strength for cleanup.

output_examples.input: User provides three product photos and asks for the same square studio background.
output_examples.output: The skill selects Nano Banana Edit, uses the batch image URL field, and locks square aspect ratio and resolution.

output_examples.input: User asks to replace an English poster headline with Japanese text.
output_examples.output: The skill selects GPT Image 2 Edit and prompts for exact text replacement while preserving layout and brand elements.

best_practices: State preservation requirements before the requested change.
Use masks for precise object removal or region replacement.
Review image URLs, prompts, and output directories before running the CLI.
anti_patterns: Do not submit private images unless third-party processing is approved.
Do not combine many unrelated edits into one prompt.
Do not use mask-driven routes without a clear grayscale mask URL.

faq.question: Does this skill edit images locally?
faq.answer: No. It uses the local RunComfy CLI to send edit requests to RunComfy model services.

faq.question: Which model does it choose by default?
faq.answer: It defaults to Nano Banana Edit when the user does not name a specific route.

faq.question: Can it process multiple images at once?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented Nano Banana route supports batch edits for up to 20 input images.

faq.question: Can it replace text inside an image?
faq.answer: Yes. It routes multilingual text replacement and layout-sensitive edits to GPT Image 2 Edit.

faq.question: Does it require image URLs?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented routes expect HTTPS URLs for source images and mask images.

faq.question: What security review is recommended?
faq.answer: Review prompts, image URLs, token handling, and output directories before running generated RunComfy commands.
